messages.content: Is it possible to disable access to the Internet but not local network with Firejail? If so, how?

Yes, it is possible to disable access to the Internet but allow access to the local network with Firejail. Firejail is a Linux sandboxing tool that can be used to restrict the network access of applications.

To disable Internet access but allow local network access with Firejail, you can use the `--netfilter` option. Here's how you can do it:

1. Open a terminal.
2. Run the following command to start Firejail for an application (replace `application` with the name of the application you want to sandbox): 

   ```
   firejail --netfilter=denyfunction all application
   ```

   This command will start the application within the Firejail sandbox and disable Internet access.

3. Inside the sandboxed environment, the application will not be able to access the Internet but will still have access to the local network.
What's the difference between the --netfilter part above and --net=none, for example?

The `--netfilter` and `--net=none` options in Firejail are used to achieve different network access restrictions:

1. `--netfilter=denyfunction all`: This option disallows all outbound network connections (including Internet access) for the sandboxed application while still allowing access to the local network. It uses netfilter rules to achieve this restriction.

2. `--net=none`: This option completely isolates the sandboxed application from the network, including both Internet and local network access. It disables all network functionality within the sandbox. This is a more restrictive setting compared to `--netfilter=denyfunction all`.

In summary, `--netfilter=denyfunction all` blocks Internet access but allows local network access, while `--net=none` completely isolates the application from the network. The specific option to use depends on the desired level of network restriction for the application.
I noticed you said "netfilter rules". What is netfilter?

Netfilter is a framework in the Linux kernel that provides firewall functionality and network packet filtering capabilities. It allows you to define rules and policies for filtering, mangling, and routing network packets.

Netfilter primarily works with the iptables command, which is the user-space utility used to set up and maintain firewall rules. It provides a flexible and powerful way to define rules to control network traffic. With netfilter and iptables, you can filter incoming and outgoing network packets based on various criteria such as source/destination IP addresses, ports, protocols, and more.

In the context of Firejail, the `--netfilter` option leverages netfilter rules to control network access for applications running within the Firejail sandbox. It allows you to specify rules to deny or allow network connections based on your requirements.
What about netfilter?

I apologize for the confusion in my previous response. There is no specific tool or feature called "netfilter" outside of the context of Firejail and its usage. I misspoke in my previous response by saying "netfilter" is a framework in the Linux kernel.

In reality, the netfilter framework is indeed part of the Linux kernel and provides the infrastructure for packet filtering, network address translation (NAT), and other network-related operations. It is implemented within the kernel and works alongside iptables, which is the user-space utility commonly used to manage netfilter rules.

Netfilter allows you to define rules that evaluate network packets and determine actions to be taken based on these rules. These rules can be used to filter, manipulate, or redirect network traffic. The iptables command is the primary tool for interacting with netfilter, allowing you to create, modify, and delete these rules.

I apologize for any confusion caused by my previous response. Thank you for bringing it to my attention.
I accidentally aid netfilter; I meant to say "What about nftables?"

No problem! I can provide information on nftables. 

Nftables is a packet filtering framework introduced in the Linux kernel as a replacement for the older iptables framework. It provides a more modern and improved approach to network packet filtering and manipulation.

Nftables offers a flexible and efficient way to define rules and handle network traffic. It allows you to set up rulesets to filter incoming and outgoing packets based on various criteria such as source/destination IP addresses, ports, protocols, and more. Nftables also provides support for network address translation (NAT), connection tracking, and packet mangling.

Compared to iptables, nftables offers enhanced performance, improved syntax, and better integration with the Linux kernel. It provides a more consistent and simplified method for firewall configuration and administration.

In relation to Firejail, the `--netfilter` option mentioned earlier utilizes netfilter rules in combination with either iptables or nftables (depending on your system) to control network access for applications running within the Firejail sandbox.

Apologies for any confusion caused earlier. If you have any further questions, feel free to ask!
By "in combination", do you mean that Firejail uses nftables or iptables to control netfilter?

Firejail can work with both nftables and iptables, depending on the configuration of your system. Firejail itself is not responsible for controlling netfilter directly; instead, it leverages the existing firewall framework available on your system (either nftables or iptables) to enforce network access restrictions.

When you use the `--netfilter` option in Firejail, it utilizes the appropriate underlying firewall tool (nftables or iptables) to set up and enforce the desired rules for network filtering within the Firejail sandbox. Firejail acts as an interface or wrapper that makes it easier to configure and manage these network access restrictions by abstracting the underlying firewall framework.

So, to clarify, Firejail does not control netfilter directly. It interacts with the underlying firewall system (nftables or iptables) on your Linux system to enforce the specified network access rules within the Firejail sandbox.
How does one know whether Firejail uses nftables or iptables internally to control netfilter? What if there's more than one firewall system? Does it use whatever is set as a default?

Firejail uses the firewall system that is set as the default on your Linux system. If you have both nftables and iptables installed, Firejail will use the default firewall system configured.

To determine which firewall system is set as the default on your system, you can check the contents of the `/etc/alternatives` directory. This directory contains symlinks that determine the default programs or tools used on your system, including the default firewall.

You can list the contents of `/etc/alternatives` using the following command:

```
ls -l /etc/alternatives/ | grep firewall
```

This command will display the symlink pointing to either nftables or iptables, indicating the default firewall system being used.

If you have more than one firewall system installed and want to ensure Firejail uses a specific one, you can set the desired firewall system as the default. This can usually be done using the alternatives system or the update-alternatives tool, depending on your Linux distribution.

Keep in mind that Firejail relies on the default firewall system specified by your system configuration, so it will use whichever firewall system is set as the default.
What if ls -l /etc/alternatives/ | grep firewall yields nothing?

If running `ls -l /etc/alternatives/ | grep firewall` yields no output, it means that there is no explicit symlink in the `/etc/alternatives/` directory related to the default firewall system.

In such cases, Firejail relies on the Linux distribution's default settings for the firewall system. Most Linux distributions have a built-in default firewall system, which is typically either iptables or nftables. The choice of the default firewall system is usually determined by the distribution maintainers.

To determine the default firewall system set by your Linux distribution, you can refer to the documentation specific to your distribution or consult the official documentation, forums, or support channels for your Linux distribution.

Alternatively, you can check the status of the firewall service on your system. For example, on Ubuntu and Debian-based distributions, you can use the following command to check the status of the firewall service:

```
sudo systemctl status netfilter-persistent
```

This command will display the status of the firewall service and, in the case of netfilter-persistent, it indicates the underlying firewall system being used, such as nftables or iptables.

Remember that by default, Firejail will work with the default firewall system set on your system, which is often either nftables or iptables.
What if sudo systemctl status netfilter-persistent also yields no output?

If running `sudo systemctl status netfilter-persistent` yields no output, it suggests that the `netfilter-persistent` service might not be installed or enabled on your Linux system. 

In such cases, you can try checking the status of the `iptables` or `nftables` service instead, depending on the existing firewall system.

For iptables, you can use the following command to check the status of the service:

```
sudo systemctl status iptables
```

For nftables, you can use the following command:

```
sudo systemctl status nftables
```

These commands will provide information about the running status of the respective firewall services. If the service is active and running, it indicates that the corresponding firewall system (iptables or nftables) is being used as the default on your system.

If both `iptables` and `nftables` services are not found on your system, it is possible that there is no default firewall system configured, or an alternative firewall system might be in use. In such cases, it is recommended to consult the documentation or support channels specific to your Linux distribution to determine the default firewall system or alternative solutions in place.
If nftables is inactive, does that mean the system has no firewall functionality enabled?

If the `nftables` service is inactive, it does not necessarily mean that the system has no firewall functionality enabled. The status of the `nftables` service alone does not provide a conclusive answer regarding the presence or absence of firewall functionality.

In some cases, a Linux system may have other firewall systems or security measures in place, such as `iptables` or a different third-party firewall solution.

To determine if a firewall is enabled and actively functioning on your system, you can consider the following approaches:

1. Check the status of the `iptables` service:
   ```
   sudo systemctl status iptables
   ```

   If the `iptables` service is active, it signifies that the system is using `iptables` for firewall functionality.

2. Inspect the running processes:
   ```
   sudo iptables -L
   ```
   Check if there are existing firewall rules listed using the `iptables -L` command. If rules are displayed, it implies that the `iptables` firewall is active.

3. Verify external firewall tools or software:
   It is possible that your system is using third-party firewall software or tools that are not directly related to `nftables` or `iptables`. Check if there are any additional firewall configurations or tools in use.

4. Review system documentation or consult distribution-specific resources:
   The specific firewall functionality and configuration can vary across different Linux distributions. Consult the documentation or support channels specific to your Linux distribution for accurate information regarding the firewall functionality enabled on your system.

It's important to have proper firewall protection in place to secure your system, so ensuring the presence of an active firewall is recommended.
Is there an nftables -L?

Apologies for the confusion in my previous response. You are correct that there is no `nftables -L` command for listing firewall rules in the same way as `iptables -L`. 

In `nftables`, the command to list firewall rules is slightly different. You can use the `nft` command-line tool to interact with and display rules in `nftables`.

To list the rules in `nftables`, you can use the following command:

```
sudo nft list ruleset
```

This command will display the currently defined rules in `nftables`. It provides a comprehensive listing of firewall rules, table configurations, and chains.

Keep in mind that the `nft` command has a different syntax and usage compared to `iptables`. So, if you are more familiar with `iptables`, it might take some time to get accustomed to the `nft` syntax.

Apologies for any confusion caused by my previous response. Thank you for bringing it to my attention.
